summary: Gruber revisits his 2013 rebuttal of Tim Wu's New Yorker piece that argued Apple's 'closed' strategy could only succeed under Steve Jobs's genius and that the company was already in decline. Wu had invented an 'openness' ranking system that allegedly correlated with market cap, which Gruber dismissed as nonsense. Five years later, Gruber notes that Apple's stock price is up 185 percent from March 2013, trading at nearly 3x the price. He uses this as vindication that the 'open beats closed' narrative was fundamentally flawed and that his original critique holds up well.
key_points: Tim Wu's 2013 theory that 'open beats closed' in tech and that Apple was declining without Jobs has been thoroughly refuted by Apple's subsequent performance
Wu's invented 'openness score' ranking system that allegedly correlated with market cap was intellectually dishonest from the start
Apple's stock price rose 185% in the five years after Wu predicted decline, demonstrating the bankruptcy of the open-vs-closed framework
The popular 2013 narrative that Samsung was eating Apple's lunch in phones proved to be completely wrong
money_quotes: I called nonsense on the whole premise that market cap correlates to openness (I called nonsense on just about every point Wu made, really).
Apple's stock price at the beginning of March 2013 was still higher than it was when Steve Jobs died.
Today, five years to the day after I wrote that, Apple's stock price is up 185 percent — it's trading at nearly 3× the price from March 2013.
It encapsulates many of the themes I've written about regarding Apple from the beginning.
Five years seems like a good time to revisit it, and I have to say my argument holds up pretty well.
key_insight: The 'open beats closed' narrative that was applied to predict Apple's post-Jobs decline has been decisively refuted by five years of market performance, demonstrating the emptiness of openness as a predictive framework.
